retrieved_docs.title: Implicit Bias -- Issues of Concern -- Persons of Nonwhite Race
retrieved_docs.content: In 2021, the Center for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC) cited racism as a serious public health threat (CDC, 2021). Indeed, numerous studies have shown specific examples of race-based discrimination in healthcare settings. For example, implicit racial biases impact clinical decision-making for pain management, noninvasive cardiac testing, thrombolysis, cardiac catheterization, and cancer screening. [33] [21] Pediatric nonwhite patients also face implicit racial biases from HCPs. [34] [35] [36] Black, Latinx, and indigenous patients are frequently met with verbal dominance from HCPs and negative experiences in the medical setting, compromising trust in HCPs and patient care quality. [37] [38] [39] HCPs who score highly on the IAT for black-white implicit bias are often rated poorly by black patients regarding patient-centered care. [37] Implicit biases against those of nonwhite race are particularly salient when the clinician perceives increased time pressure and ambiguity, such as in acute care or emergencies. [40] [41] [42] The COVID-19 pandemic exacerbated discriminatory attitudes towards HCPs of Asian and Pacific Islander descent. [43] [44]

retrieved_docs.title: Diversity Goals for Evaluation and Treatment of American Indians and Alaska Natives -- Clinical Significance -- Mortality Disparity Rates
retrieved_docs.content: Disparities in mortality rates emerge in infancy and continue throughout development for AIAN populations. Rates of inadequate prenatal care and post-natal death are more than 2 times higher for AIAN infants than the general United States infant population. [60]  AIAN infants are more likely to die from sudden infant death syndrome, pneumonia, influenza, accidents, and homicide. [57] AIAN children between 1 and 4 years have 3 times the death rate of children in the general population. AIAN youth are 2 times more likely to experience an injury-related death due to motor vehicle accidents, pedestrian events, and suicide compared to the general United States population. [57] More recent reports estimate that AIAN adolescents (aged 15 to 24) suicide rates are 4 times greater than non-Hispanic White adolescent suicide rates. [56]

retrieved_docs.title: Anterior Myocardial Infarction -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: Seeking emergency medical service as soon as possible is crucial for improving outcomes in anterior myocardial infarction. Therefore, upon arrival, obtaining an ECG is of the utmost importance, and guidelines recommend patients with suspected AMI should receive an ECG within 10 minutes of the first medical encounter.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 2559
retrieved_docs.content: Acute Q wave infarction – widespread, acute ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarctions caused by occlusion of the distal left anterior descending coronary artery, the circumflex branch of the left coronary artery, the distal portion of the right coronary artery, the intermediate artery, the marginal branch of the right coronary artery, or the posterior descending branch of the right coronary artery, as well as acute ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarction involving the left anterior descending coronary artery and acute myocardial infarction due to left coronary artery occlusion, all represent distinct presentations of an acute myocardial infarction that share a common pathological substrate in the myocardium. Acute non‑Q wave infarction, acute non‑Q wave infarction – widespread, acute non‑ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarction (disorder), acute widespread myocardial infarction, postoperative acute myocardial infarction, and subsequent anterior non‑ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarction are additional variants of acute myocardial infarction, each reflecting different electrocardiographic patterns, timing, or clinical contexts, yet each also localizes its injury to the myocardial tissue. Collectively, these entities illustrate how diverse coronary artery occlusions, ECG morphologies, and clinical settings converge on myocardial damage as the central finding site, underscoring the importance of recognizing the specific type of infarction for appropriate diagnosis, risk stratification, and management in patient care.

retrieved_docs.content: Acute myocardial infarction is classified as encompassing the subgroup of thrombotic and arterial embolism cases (حالات الخثار والانصمام الشريانية, SMQ), indicating that these thrombotic and embolic arterial events are considered members of the acute myocardial infarction category. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 3 -- Community 2197
retrieved_docs.content: Acute infarct is a cardiac pathology that can manifest with a wide range of morphologic patterns. It is associated with acute Q wave infarction – including the widespread form – and with acute Q wave myocardial infarction. Various forms of acute ST segment elevation myocardial infarction are also linked to acute infarct, such as the general disorder and specific presentations caused by occlusion of distinct coronary segments: the anterior descending branch of the left coronary artery, the distal portion of the right coronary artery, the mid portion of the right coronary artery, the posterior descending branch of the right coronary artery, and right coronary artery occlusion overall. In addition, acute infarct encompasses acute myocardial infarction due to occlusion of the circumflex branch of the left coronary artery, acute myocardial infarction due to right coronary artery occlusion, and acute myocardial infarction that occurs during a medical procedure. Non‑Q wave morphologies are included as well, with both acute non‑Q wave infarction and its widespread variant being associated with acute infarct. Furthermore, acute infarct is linked to acute non‑ST segment elevation myocardial infarction and to subsequent anterior non‑ST segment elevation myocardial infarction, reflecting its relevance to both initial and later ischemic events. Together, these associations illustrate that acute infarct can present with diverse electrocardiographic and anatomic features, emphasizing the importance of recognizing its various morphologies for accurate diagnosis and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 4 -- Community 2914
retrieved_docs.content: Chest pain (ألم الصَدْر) is clinically associated with cardiac catheterization (قسطرة قلبية). In clinical practice, the presence of chest pain often prompts consideration of cardiac catheterization to evaluate coronary artery disease, while the procedure itself may produce chest discomfort as a post‑procedural symptom. Recognizing this association helps clinicians diagnose underlying cardiac pathology and manage patient symptoms before, during, and after the intervention.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 2136
retrieved_docs.content: Acute myocardial infarction can present in many distinct clinical and electrocardiographic forms, yet all of these entities share the common pathological involvement of the myocardium. The condition includes classic presentations such as Acute Q‑wave myocardial infarction and Acute non‑Q‑wave infarction – widespread, as well as various ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarctions (STEMI) that are defined by the specific coronary artery segment that is occluded. Examples of STEMI subtypes are Acute ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarction due to occlusion of the anterior descending branch of the left coronary artery, the circumflex branch of the left coronary artery, the proximal, distal, marginal, septal, or posterior descending branches of the right coronary artery, and the proximal left anterior descending coronary artery. Additional classifications encompass Acute ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarction involving the left anterior descending coronary artery, Acute myocardial infarction due to left coronary artery occlusion, Acute myocardial infarction due to occlusion of the circumflex branch of the left coronary artery, and Acute ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarction due to occlusion of the septal branch of the anterior descending branch of the left coronary artery. Non‑ST‑segment elevation presentations are also represented, such as Subsequent anterior non‑ST segment elevation myocardial infarction. Postoperative settings can give rise to Postoperative acute myocardial infarction. Despite the diversity of terminology, anatomical location, and timing, each of these acute coronary syndromes is characterized by injury to the myokard (myocardial tissue). Recognizing that the myocardium is the universal site of injury across all these variants is essential for accurate diagnosis, risk stratification, and the selection of appropriate therapeutic strategies in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 2364
retrieved_docs.content: coronary artery, and Acute ST segment elevation myocardial infarction due to distal portion of the right coronary artery all share the acute infarct morphology. In addition, acute infarct morphology is also associated with Acute myocardial infarction due to left coronary artery occlusion, Acute myocardial infarction due to right coronary artery occlusion, Acute non‑ST segment elevation myocardial infarction (NSTEMI), and Subsequent anterior non‑ST segment elevation myocardial infarction. Together, these relationships illustrate that acute infarct serves as the common pathological substrate linking diverse coronary occlusion sites and both ST‑segment elevation and non‑ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarction presentations, which is essential for accurate diagnosis, risk stratification, and targeted therapeutic management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 4 -- Community 2909
retrieved_docs.content: Chest pain (ألم الصَدْر) and increased blood pressure (زيادة ضغط الدم) are both listed as members of the myocardial infarction (اعتِلاَل عَضَل القَلب) Standardized MedDRA Query (SMQ). This relationship indicates that chest pain and elevated blood pressure are clinical features that can be associated with myocardial infarction, highlighting their relevance for the recognition, assessment, and management of this cardiac condition.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 1890
retrieved_docs.content: Acute infarct serves as a unifying morphological descriptor that links a wide spectrum of acute myocardial infarction entities. Acute Q wave infarction – widespread is identified as having the morphology of an acute infarct. Numerous acute ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarctions, each defined by the specific coronary artery or branch that is occluded—such as mid left anterior descending coronary artery, circumflex branch of the left coronary artery, diagonal branch of the anterior descending branch, intermediate artery, mid portion of the right coronary artery, posterior lateral branch of the right coronary artery, proximal portion of the right coronary artery, septal branch of the anterior descending branch, proximal left anterior descending coronary artery, and right coronary artery occlusion—are all described as having the associated morphology of an acute infarct. In addition, the broader categories of acute myocardial infarction due to left coronary artery occlusion, acute myocardial infarction due to right coronary artery occlusion, and acute non‑ST segment elevation myocardial infarction are likewise linked to the acute infarct morphology. This integrated classification highlights that despite diverse anatomic locations and electrocardiographic presentations, these conditions share the underlying pathophysiology of an acute infarct, which is essential for accurate diagnosis, risk assessment, and targeted patient care.
